text.title: Browser Automation Tool Selection — Puppeteer vs Playwright
text.summary: Feature comparison matrix, concrete when-to-use rules, minimal code comparison, and a scoring script for tool routing.

rule.statement: Choose Playwright when cross-browser coverage, built-in auto-waiting, video/trace recording, or accessibility (role) selectors are required; choose Puppeteer for Chrome-only scraping, PDF/screenshot generation, or CDP-level access.
rule.rationale: Playwright adds cross-browser abstraction and auto-wait at the cost of slightly higher setup complexity; Puppeteer's simpler API is appropriate when only Chromium is needed and auto-wait is not a priority.

list.item: Browsers: Puppeteer = Chrome/Chromium only; Playwright = Chrome, Firefox, WebKit
Auto-waiting: Puppeteer = manual waitForSelector; Playwright = built-in on all actions
Testing framework: Puppeteer = separate (Jest/Mocha); Playwright = built-in (@playwright/test)
Selector types: Puppeteer = CSS/XPath; Playwright = CSS, XPath, text, role (accessibility)
Video/tracing: Puppeteer = manual CDP; Playwright = built-in, one config flag

section.title: Routing Script
section.reference: Scoring script: pick Puppeteer vs Playwright based on boolean requirement flags.

rule.statement: After choosing a tool from this overview, always load the sibling methodology (puppeteer-automation/, playwright-automation/, or web-scraping/) before producing any automation code.
rule.rationale: This file is a router with ~100 lines of context. The sibling files contain the actual patterns, selectors, anti-detection, Page Object Model, and CI integration details needed for production code.
